question: Matthew 28:19: why is the singular ‘name’ with threefold Father/Son/Spirit a compact Trinitarian clue—and how do you defend it against ‘it should say names’ nitpicking?

choices.A: ‘Name’ can function as a singular marker of shared identity/authority; the triadic formula unites three persons under one ‘name.’
choices.B: It is a scribal error and should be removed.
choices.C: It teaches three unrelated gods with separate names.
choices.D: It proves Father, Son, and Spirit are the same person.

teaching.title: One ‘name,’ three persons
teaching.body: The grammar is striking: one ‘name’ paired with Father, Son, and Holy Spirit. Apologetically, it functions as compressed Trinitarian data—unity of divine identity/authority with real personal distinction—without needing later philosophical vocabulary to see the pattern.
